name: session_capacity_heavy
description: 999 agents (just under m_maxSessions=1000) running long heavy sessions: 2000 DataValues × ~8 KB each, total ~16 MB per session. The hypothesis is that at session capacity + heavy per-session load, the workers can't drain m_workersQueue/m_indexerQueue fast enough and RAM grows. Compare against sustained_high_rate (300 agents × 200 DataValues, ~120 KB per session): this scenario pushes ~135x more data per session AND saturates m_agentSessions. Note: functionally equivalent to 333 real Wazuh agents running 3 modules each in parallel — the manager only sees the global session count and per-session work, not who owns the session.
notes: Hypothesis: with m_agentSessions at capacity (~999/1000) AND each session writing 2000×8KB=16MB to RocksDB + pushing 2000 Response{} into m_indexerQueue, the workers may not keep up. If RAM grows monotonically during the run, the queues are retaining work. If RAM oscillates in a sawtooth like sustained_high_rate did, the system handles it.
Why 999 agents (not 1000+): we want to stress at capacity, not trigger the session_limit_reached guard rail. mega_burst@2000 already tested the guard rail path.
Why data_size=2000: ~10x what sustained_high_rate used. Each session takes longer to complete, so sessions stay in m_agentSessions longer, keeping the count near capacity for a sustained period.
Why payload_size=8192: well under OS_MAXSTR=65536. The pad goes into file.path (PAD_FIELD_BY_KIND['fim_file']). Each DataValue's JSON is ~8 KB; the FB wrapper adds ~200 bytes per message.
- If sawtooth in 200-400 MB band → workers drain at capacity, no real queue risk.
- If monotonic ramp >500 MB → unbounded growth confirmed at capacity + heavy work.
- Tail latency p99 will be high regardless of outcome (manager is at capacity).
